name: Request Task PIC and Collaborators
description: Answers questions about who is responsible for, assigned to, working on, owning, or blocking a request or request task. Use when the user asks who the PIC (person in charge), owner, assignee, or collaborator is, which users are involved with a task, who is blocking progress, or who is working on a given request or DOR. The primary owner of each request task lives in request_tasks.pic_id (a users.id), and additional helpers live in request_task_collaborators.user_id — both groups should normally be returned together with a UNION. Filter request_tasks.status = 'in_progress' when the question is specifically about who is currently blocking progress, since only in-progress tasks can block. Related phrasings: responsible user, assigned to, assignee, owner, in charge, person in charge, PIC, collaborator, working on, blocking, in progress, request, request task, DOR, Diag-on-Request.
sql: SELECT users.name FROM request_tasks JOIN users ON request_tasks.pic_id = users.id WHERE request_tasks.request_id = 50000 AND request_tasks.status = 'in_progress' UNION SELECT users.name FROM request_tasks JOIN request_task_collaborators ON request_task_collaborators.request_task_id = request_tasks.id JOIN users ON request_task_collaborators.user_id = users.id WHERE request_tasks.request_id = 50000 AND request_tasks.status = 'in_progress'

name: Task Frequency by Type
description: Disambiguates between the tasks table (task definitions / task types / templates — a small fixed set of named task kinds such as 'Diag Review', 'GPU MODS', 'Log Review', 'SharePoint', 'Nautilus', 'OneDiag', 'BOM Coverage') and the request_tasks table (one row per task instance attached to a specific request). Use this analysis when the user asks about task frequency, most common tasks, rollback counts per task type, distribution of task types, 'tasks by name', or any aggregation over task types — those questions need the tasks table joined through the chain request_task_events → request_tasks → filter_rule_group_tasks → workflow_tasks → tasks. Do NOT join to tasks when the question is about task instances themselves — assignees, collaborators, statuses, percentages of tasks with some property, in-progress tasks — those live entirely on request_tasks and joining to tasks just adds noise. Important: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM tasks counts task definitions, not task instances. Related phrasings: task definition, task template, task type, task name, task instance, task per request, rollback frequency, common task, most frequent task, percentage of tasks, tasks with collaborators, pending tasks, in-progress tasks.
sql: SELECT tasks.name, COUNT(*) AS event_count FROM request_task_events JOIN request_tasks ON request_task_events.request_task_id = request_tasks.id JOIN filter_rule_group_tasks ON request_tasks.filter_rule_group_task_id = filter_rule_group_tasks.id JOIN workflow_tasks ON filter_rule_group_tasks.workflow_task_id = workflow_tasks.id JOIN tasks ON workflow_tasks.task_id = tasks.id GROUP BY tasks.name ORDER BY event_count DESC LIMIT 10

name: Request Trainstop, Quality, Process
description: Answers questions about a request's trainstop (also called milestone or train stop), quality level (DVT, EVT, MP0, MP1, MP2, POR, QS), or process / process code by name (e.g. AMP, FLT, GPU, MOD, SLT). The requests table stores only foreign-key IDs for these attributes — to get the human-readable name you must join trainstops on requests.trainstop_id = trainstops.id, quality_levels on requests.quality_level_id = quality_levels.id, and processes through the request_processes link table (request_processes.request_id = requests.id AND request_processes.process_id = processes.id). Important: although requests has a process_ids array column, in practice the canonical many-to-many link goes through request_processes — always use the join table for the process relationship. Use when the user mentions a request's trainstop, milestone, quality level, quality stage, process, or asks 'which trainstop / quality level / process is request X at'. Related phrasings: trainstop, train stop, milestone, quality level, quality, quality stage, process, process name, process code, request, DOR.
sql: SELECT requests.id AS request_id, trainstops.name AS trainstop, quality_levels.name AS quality_level, processes.name AS process FROM requests JOIN trainstops ON requests.trainstop_id = trainstops.id JOIN quality_levels ON requests.quality_level_id = quality_levels.id JOIN request_processes ON request_processes.request_id = requests.id JOIN processes ON processes.id = request_processes.process_id WHERE requests.status = 'in_progress'

name: Pcode, Product, BU Hierarchy
description: Answers questions about the pcode → product → business unit (BU) hierarchy at NVIDIA. A pcode (product code such as PG153, P3737, GB110, E3931) belongs to a product (Quadro, GeForce, Blackwell, Hopper, Tegra, Jetson, Tesla, etc.) via pcodes.product_id = products.id, and each product belongs to a business unit (GPU, Auto, Embedded, Server, Tesla, Shield, SLT, Tester) via products.bu_id = bus.id. Use this when the user asks which BU owns a pcode, which products belong to a BU, how many BUs a pcode appears in, distribution of pcodes by business unit, the product for a given pcode, or any product / BU mapping. Important: the same pcode name can appear as multiple rows linked to different products and BUs — when aggregating across the hierarchy, GROUP BY pcodes.name (not pcodes.id), otherwise each row is treated independently and cross-BU matches are hidden. Related phrasings: pcode, product code, product, business unit, BU, hierarchy, GPU product, Quadro, GeForce, Blackwell, NVIDIA product line.
sql: SELECT pcodes.name AS pcode, COUNT(DISTINCT bus.id) AS bu_count, COUNT(DISTINCT products.id) AS product_count FROM pcodes JOIN products ON pcodes.product_id = products.id JOIN bus ON products.bu_id = bus.id GROUP BY pcodes.name ORDER BY bu_count DESC LIMIT 20

name: Percent of Tasks with Collaborators
description: Computes the percentage of request tasks (request_tasks) that have at least one collaborator (request_task_collaborators) — i.e. how many request tasks are shared work versus assigned to a single PIC only. Use whenever the user asks for the share / percentage / ratio / fraction of request tasks that have collaborators, the proportion of tasks worked on by more than one user, or any 'how many / what % of request tasks have collaborators' question. The same query shape generalizes to any 'percentage / ratio of rows in table A that have a matching row in related table B' question, and to conditional counting / frequency by category that must include zero-count groups — joining request_tasks LEFT JOIN request_task_collaborators ON request_task_collaborators.request_task_id = request_tasks.id, then dividing COUNT(DISTINCT request_tasks.id) FILTER (WHERE request_task_collaborators.id IS NOT NULL) by COUNT(DISTINCT request_tasks.id). Two technical reasons to use this shape: (a) COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE …) keeps groups with zero matches (WHERE would drop them); (b) over a LEFT JOIN, raw COUNT(*) overcounts when the right side has many matches, so COUNT(DISTINCT request_tasks.id) is required in both numerator and denominator. Tables: request_tasks, request_task_collaborators (joined on request_task_collaborators.request_task_id = request_tasks.id). Related phrasings: percent of request tasks with collaborators, percentage of tasks with collaborators, share of tasks that have collaborators, ratio of tasks with collaborators, fraction of tasks with collaborators, conditional count, percentage with FILTER WHERE, percentage with COUNT DISTINCT, percentage over LEFT JOIN.
sql: SELECT 100.0 * COUNT(DISTINCT request_tasks.id) FILTER (WHERE request_task_collaborators.id IS NOT NULL) / COUNT(DISTINCT request_tasks.id) AS pct_tasks_with_collaborators FROM request_tasks LEFT JOIN request_task_collaborators ON request_task_collaborators.request_task_id = request_tasks.id

name: GPU MODS Version and Fields
description: Answers questions about MODS-related values that users selected when filling out request tasks: GPU MODS version, MODS branch, MODS architecture, MODS trace files, Tegra MODS files, or any other MODS / diagnostic-version field of a request. These task-field values live in the EAV table request_task_fields, keyed by request_id, request_task_id, and field_name; the value itself is in text_value, numeric_value, boolean_value, or array_value depending on field type. The relevant field_name values are 'modsVersion' (the version string, in text_value), 'modsBranch' (the branch name, in text_value), 'modsArchitecture' (in text_value), 'modsTraceFiles' (in array_value), and 'tegraModsFiles' (in array_value). Use whenever the user asks about MODS version distribution, which MODS versions are used, count of requests per MODS version, MODS branch in use, 'what GPU MODS version is request X using', or any question that pivots on MODS-related task fields. Related phrasings: GPU MODS, MODS, modsVersion, MODS version, MODS branch, MODS architecture, Tegra MODS, diagnostics version, diag version.
sql: SELECT request_attribute_values.text_value AS mods_version, COUNT(*) AS request_count FROM request_attribute_values WHERE request_attribute_values.field_name = 'modsVersion' GROUP BY request_attribute_values.text_value ORDER BY request_count DESC
